Name: CurvatureAwarePrecisionRouting
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing for Efficient and Stable Mixed-Precision Training
Short Hypothesis: By developing a curvature-aware precision routing (CAPR) framework, we can dynamically assign mixed-precision levels to layers based on their curvature and gradient variance, leading to more efficient and stable training of deep neural networks while maintaining or improving model accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Tri-Accel [7] and SNIP [3] have explored mixed-precision training and quantization, but they do not fully leverage the geometric properties of the loss landscape to guide precision assignment. EMQ [1] focuses on training-free proxies for mixed-precision quantization, but it does not address the dynamic adjustment of precision during training. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating curvature and gradient variance diagnostics into a precision routing framework, ensuring both efficiency and stability.
Abstract: Mixed-precision training has become a crucial technique for optimizing the computational efficiency of deep neural networks. However, existing methods often rely on heuristic-based precision assignment or static configurations, which can lead to suboptimal performance and stability issues. In this work, we propose a Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR) framework that dynamically assigns mixed-precision levels to layers based on their curvature and gradient variance. By leveraging geometric subspace diagnostics, CAPR ensures that layers with higher curvature and gradient variance are assigned higher precision, while less sensitive layers use lower precision. This approach not only reduces computational overhead but also maintains or improves model accuracy. We validate CAPR on various deep learning tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving a better accuracy-complexity trade-off compared to existing methods.
Experiments: Layer-wise Curvature and Gradient Variance Analysis: Perform a detailed analysis of the curvature and gradient variance for each layer in the network. This involves computing the Hessian trace and gradient covariance for each layer during the initial training phase.

Dynamic Precision Assignment: Develop a precision assignment algorithm that dynamically adjusts the precision of each layer based on the computed curvature and gradient variance. This algorithm will use a threshold-based approach to determine the precision level (e.g., FP32, FP16, INT8) for each layer.

Training Stability and Convergence Analysis: Train the model using the CAPR framework and compare its stability and convergence with baseline mixed-precision training methods. Track metrics such as training loss, validation accuracy, and computational overhead.

Ablation Studies: Conduct ablation studies to evaluate the impact of different precision assignment strategies and threshold values. This will help in understanding the sensitivity of the model to precision changes.

Evaluation on Benchmarks: Evaluate the performance of CAPR on standard benchmarks such as CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and LLMs. Compare the results with state-of-the-art mixed-precision training methods in terms of accuracy, memory usage, and computational efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk is the computational overhead associated with the curvature and gradient variance analysis, which could slow down the initial training phase. However, this overhead can be mitigated by performing the analysis at specific intervals or using approximate methods. Another limitation is the sensitivity of the precision assignment to the choice of thresholds, which may require fine-tuning for different models and datasets. Additionally, the effectiveness of CAPR may vary across different architectures and tasks, necessitating thorough validation on a diverse set of benchmarks.
